counting-related-rows-without-inflation.label: Counting Related Rows Without Inflation
counting-related-rows-without-inflation.summary: Learn to count matched rows and unique entities accurately in SQL without allowing join multiplication to distort reports.

sketch0.title: Count the Related Row, Not the Placeholder

sketch1.title: Count Entities Once at the Intended Grain

try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.title: Try Counting Enrollments per Student
try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.prompt: List every student and the number of enrollment rows they have. Use LEFT JOIN so Liam appears with an enrollment count of zero.
try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.hint: Count a nullable identifier from the right table, not `COUNT(*)`.

help.concept: Counting the right-side primary key produces zero for an unmatched LEFT JOIN row.
help.hint_1: Group at one row per student and count `enrollments.id`.
help.hint_2: Start from `students`; the lesson example instead counts courses per department.

try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.solutionCode:
SELECT students.name,
       COUNT(enrollments.id) AS enrollment_count
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
GROUP BY students.id, students.name
ORDER BY students.id;

try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.title: Try Counting Department Breadth per Student
try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.prompt: List every student and the number of distinct departments represented by their enrolled courses. Preserve Liam with a department count of zero.
try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.hint: Follow enrollments to courses, then count unique department ids at the student grain.

help.concept: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) can measure breadth while preventing repeated related rows from inflating a metric.
help.hint_1: Group by the student and count `DISTINCT courses.department_id`.
help.hint_2: The lesson counts unique students per department; this exercise reverses the grain to departments per student.

try_counting_related_rows_without_inflat.solutionCode:
SELECT students.name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT courses.department_id) AS department_count
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
LEFT JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id
GROUP BY students.id, students.name
ORDER BY students.id;

quiz1.title: Purpose of COUNT with LEFT JOIN
quiz1.prompt: What is the purpose of using COUNT(column) with a LEFT JOIN?
quiz1.hint: Consider how COUNT(column) handles NULL values.

help.concept: COUNT(column) with LEFT JOIN counts non-NULL values, preserving unmatched rows.
help.hint_1: COUNT(column) ignores NULLs, which are common in LEFT JOIN results.
help.hint_2: Using COUNT(column) ensures unmatched rows are counted as zero.

options.a: To count all rows, including NULLs

quiz2.title: Benefits of COUNT(DISTINCT)
quiz2.prompt: What are the benefits of using COUNT(DISTINCT column) in SQL? Select all that apply.
quiz2.hint: Think about how COUNT(DISTINCT) affects duplicate values.

help.concept: COUNT(DISTINCT) counts unique values, reducing duplicates.
help.hint_1: It helps in counting unique entities in a dataset.
help.hint_2: It prevents duplicate values from inflating counts.

quiz3.title: Plan a Join-Aware Count
quiz3.prompt: Arrange the checks for building a grouped count that preserves empty groups and avoids duplicate inflation.
quiz3.hint: Define the metric and result grain before choosing the count expression.

help.concept: A trustworthy joined count begins with the reporting grain and the entity being counted.
help.hint_1: Preserve the requested groups before adding detail tables.
help.hint_2: Use a right-side identifier for related rows and `DISTINCT` when the metric is unique entities.

tokens.t1: State what one result row represents
tokens.t2: Choose the starting table whose groups must remain
tokens.t4: Count the identifier that matches the requested metric
tokens.t5: Verify zero groups and repeated entities in the result

quiz4.title: COUNT with NULL Handling
quiz4.prompt: In a LEFT JOIN, using COUNT(column) will count only the [blank1] values.
quiz4.hint: Consider what COUNT(column) does with NULLs.

help.concept: COUNT(column) counts the missing term values in a result set.
help.hint_1: COUNT(column) ignores NULLs, focusing on actual data.
help.hint_2: NULLs are not counted by COUNT(column).

quiz4.template: In a LEFT JOIN, using COUNT(column) will count only the [blank1] values.

quiz5.title: Purpose of COUNT(DISTINCT)
quiz5.prompt: The purpose of COUNT(DISTINCT column) is to count [blank1] values.
quiz5.hint: Think about how COUNT(DISTINCT) handles duplicates.

help.concept: COUNT(DISTINCT) counts the missing term values, excluding duplicates.
help.hint_1: It focuses on the missing term entries in a dataset.
help.hint_2: Duplicates are not counted by COUNT(DISTINCT).

quiz5.template: The purpose of COUNT(DISTINCT column) is to count [blank1] values.

quiz6.title: Understanding Report Grain
quiz6.prompt: Why is it important to state the grouped result grain before choosing a count expression? Select all that apply.
quiz6.hint: Consider how the result grain affects the interpretation of counts.

help.concept: The result grain defines what each row represents, affecting count accuracy.
help.hint_1: The grain determines the level of detail in the report.
help.hint_2: Choosing the wrong grain can lead to misleading counts.

options.b: To define the level of detail

sketch0.title: COUNT(right_table.id) turns unmatched rows into zero
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
An outer join preserves a left-side row even when no related row exists. That preserved row still participates in grouping, so the expression inside `COUNT` determines whether the missing relationship becomes zero or one.

```sql
SELECT departments.name,
       COUNT(courses.id) AS course_count
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses
  ON departments.id = courses.department_id
GROUP BY departments.id, departments.name;
```

The report has one group per department. For a department with courses, `courses.id` is non-`NULL` once for each matched course. For a department without courses, the preserved row contains `NULL` in `courses.id`, and `COUNT(courses.id)` ignores it. The result is zero.

`COUNT(*)` would answer a different question. It counts every joined row, including the placeholder row created for an unmatched department, so that department would incorrectly appear to have one related row.

Name the metric before choosing the expression. If the metric is “number of courses,” count a column that exists only when a course row exists. A non-null right-side primary key is usually the clearest evidence of a real match.

sketch1.title: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) protects unique-entity metrics
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Longer join paths can repeat the same entity. A student taking two courses in one department creates two enrollment rows, so counting `enrollments.student_id` measures enrollments, not unique students. **Duplicate inflation** occurs when the joined grain is more detailed than the entity the metric is supposed to count.

```sql
SELECT departments.name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT enrollments.student_id) AS unique_students
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses
  ON departments.id = courses.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments
  ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id
GROUP BY departments.id, departments.name;
```

One result row represents one department because of the grouping. Inside each group, `COUNT(DISTINCT enrollments.student_id)` collapses repeated appearances of the same student before counting. A student enrolled in two Humanities courses contributes one unique student, not two.

`DISTINCT` is not a general cleanup switch. Use it when the metric explicitly asks for unique entities and the relationship path can repeat their identifiers. If the metric asks for enrollment rows, ordinary `COUNT(enrollments.id)` is correct.

Reliable counts begin with two sentences: “one joined row represents ___” and “the metric counts unique ___.” The gap between those grains tells you whether `DISTINCT` is needed.
